author: @timeofthenick
text:
Of course the argument can occasionally (commonly in my case) make someone else just try... Just consider a different perspective to try something, not because you're trying to win the argument, but because you want to continue to tinker on an idea. 

I honestly don't really understand anyone that can know something as law, but I do occasionally appreciate it as it may make me question something... And in my opinion, that's worth a lot to me. 

I grew up knowing that someone can always be smarter, ideas may (and often do) differ, science is allowed to sway and that the most extraordinary people can accept that they can't fully understand everything.

Thank you sir, I appreciate these ones quite a lot.

author: @danedmiston9673
text: If a wind turbine is to be used for power usage then it would be useful to know the "Wind Turbine Power Coefficient".  An exact percentage is not as important as having a method to compare the efficiency of one turbine design over another for any given wind speed.  Without an efficiency comparison there is no way of knowing the cost effectiveness (in time or money) of a design.

author: @JankyShack
text: I'm biased, but my favorite wind turbine is a Siemens 2.3MW because I spent 6 years commissioning them. However that being said I live in Florida, USA and the average wind speed here is ~5m/s so I decided to go for solar panels instead of wind turbines.  You should find out the average wind speed in your area at your PLANNED HUB HEIGHT, since industrial turbines are 80m tall and wind speed generally increases as altitude increases. I also have the luxury of tropical storms and hurricanes so unfortunately no wind turbines for me :(  Keep the building and experimentation going!

author: @EdwardRLyons
text:
In amateur astronomy there's an easy answer to the question: "What's the best telescope I can buy?"

Answer: "The best telescope is the one you will use."

There are plenty of examples of people who buy an all singing, all dancing, computer controlled telescope with the most bells and whistles on it that money can buy. Then they end up never using it because it's too big, or too complex, or too heavy, or they live in a high-rise with nowhere to put it. So, was it really the "best" telescope for them?

author: @GreggGermain-lk8if
text:
Perhaps there is a misunderstanding about the question of "best"

You have made a bamboo turbine, rose turbine, while back you made one similar to the bamboo but using lengths of PVC pipe cut into quarters, you've made vertical turbines out of washing machine tubs, out of 55 gallon drums...etc.  And as you say, you can't know my local situation regarding materials, winds, my skills, availability of materials etc.

I can judge for myself how much work they are to make and whether or not the materials are available to me.  I also know the behavior of the wind in my locale and what skills I possess.

What I want to know is a measure of the efficiency of turning a given speed of wind into RPM.  Or, really, rpm across a range of speeds. 

That way *I* cam make the trade off between availability of materials, efficacy given the local winds, how much work is involved, what materials are available to me, etc.and select the one that best suits me. You can't be expected to know enough to do that as you say.

 For example if the bamboo turbine is a little better at generating an RPM across the wind speeds I care about, than, say the PVC bladed turbine, BUT the PVC is easier for me to find as opposed to  large diameter bamboo, then I might make that trade.

I don't have the time or materials available to make all of the turbines you've made and measure  the rpm  efficiency of each one under different wind conditions.

I suspect you don't have the time to make those graphs either. So perhaps each of us can select one, build it and make a graph of wind speed vs rpm.

When you do demo a turbine in your videos, you always tell us what the speed of the wind is. in addition to telling us volts and amps. So, if you could you somehow measure,  the RPM and report it, that will supply for all of us one data point.

Thanks - love the videos.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text:
Large scale wind turbines tend to have wind speeds roughly between 10 to 55 mph before breaking is needed.  I personally am interested ONLY in the max amount of watts a turbine can produce.  I always want to keep cost down but not willing to double cost to squeek out a few extra watts so common sense does come into play.  Neighbors is not a concern in large scale.  Blade design for wind speed is paramount for watt output.  Longevity is also important as common sense dictates the time value of money.  Lasting 20+ years is important financially as it lowers cost long term.  
WITH THAT SAID, what shape, angle, size, material is ideal?  That would be my goal.  Knowing what others are looking for, budget and what they are trying to do is important.  Mute point?

author: @hempev

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
shading the forward section usually does help, and you can arrange a shield/deflector to track the wind so no need for consistent wind direction, and vawts don't care anyway. but the aerodynamics can vary a lot with some rather small details of the turbine and the shield chosen. try it.
other types of 'deflectors' are available.   ;-)
also try with HAWT on a wind-seeking stand as well (I mean hawt as in barrel-type), I put a test one inside a shield shaped like a shed roof so that it projected out of where the ridge would have been, it worked ok but was very touchy about wind direction. but it did make me think about a 'scoop' type shield, though I never built that one.

The ROI approach reminds me of that proverb about the best time to plant a tree. 
I've planted plenty, under the shade of which I'll never sit. 

Imagine if we each did something, every day, even a little something, meant to make things better for others. 
What if most of those somethings didn't work out? 
Now, just imagine, what would be left of that what did? 

I'll say it to myself too:
Go make something! Let's make something!

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: We know your wind turbines are cheap and low skill, but we cant make any judgement on the designs as you never seem to test any of them. By test I mean stick them up on a mast for a few months and then summarise the issues you have seen (survivability/vibration/design issues etc etc) and publish the total power produced over the test period. My selection process always involves reviews, tests, facts and figures and it would be nice to see your designs tested. At the moment (and for the last 20 years) there is only one choice for DIY wind turbines,  High Piggott designs. I/many others would be happy to long term test your prototypes against a commercial unit or other DIY design, if you dont have the time.

author: @miketipping1583
text: I know it's a small point but... If you're by the sea the wind changes direction twice a day. From the land  to the sea at night and the sea to the land during the day, powered by the sea warming up and cooling down faster than the land. So you need your wind turbine to be turnable by the sea probably more than any other place.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: or two cheap ones facing opposite directions - two roses?

replies.author: @mikeguitar9769
replies.text: You mean: powered by the *land* warming up and cooling down faster than the *sea* .

replies.text: @mikeguitar9769 hmmm... land does warm up faster but it also retains the temperature better so is more stable than the sea that heats and cools more on a day / night cycle.... that's 30 year old memory of geography though so you may be right.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: I tinkered with that idea, using the tension on the working section of chain against a spring to automatically change gear. I had to add an intermediate flywheel to get it to work 'smoothly' though. I had some other problems with it too. But give it a go. There are lots of ways to do it. I found altering the pitch of the blades quite effective, centrifugal mass against spring tension worked well and was fairly easy to do.

replies.author: @Beeeeeeeeeee
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Commenting on something always sounds a lot more harsh than I mean... The issue with bike gearing and a derailleur is that they don't like being switched under load at all. Bike chains also tend to stretch a lot in comparison to bigger chains. Quite some maintenance will be needed, and the energy needed to produce a steel bike chain is much more than you'll ever get out of it. I'm all for experimenting, but a thorough analysis in advance can save a lot of cost/emissions/waste materials.

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: The best Wind Gene is one that best suits the environment/position it is going to be placed in, and is the easiest for everyday folk to build. As we urgently need a mass movement of DIY renewable energy wombles, so we can stop the unconscionable & unsustainable chucking out of millions of Motors in our Appliances & Device’s every year. It is utter madness to chuck all these motors away, only to then build mega centralised Wind/Solar ‘farms’. We need to stop manufacturing stuff and learn to utilise what already exists, to create the beginnings of a Cyclic/regenerative Economy to replace the current unsustainable linear Growth Economy!
